pattern: Connecting equal values without crossings is exactly a longest common subsequence, so a two-row dynamic program over the arrays gives the count.
hints: A non-crossing matching keeps the chosen indices increasing on both rows at once — that is a familiar subsequence condition.
Think of building the answer from prefixes: dp[i][j] is the best you can do using the first i of one array and first j of the other.
When the current pair of values is equal, extend the diagonal subproblem by one; otherwise carry the better of dropping one element from either side.

edge_inputs.description: small case where matching one pair blocks another
shared values but order forces a choice

stress.description: moderate arrays over a tiny alphabet so many matches and crossings appear
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    m = max(1, size - rng.randint(0, size // 3))
    a = [rng.randint(1, 6) for _ in range(n)]
    b = [rng.randint(1, 6) for _ in range(m)]
    return [a, b]
